dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 78, responding for a fainting call at a break room in downtown [CITY].
Hello, I'm Mark, an EMT with the rescue squad. What specifically brought you to call 911 this morning?
I started feeling faint and very sweaty about 30 minutes ago. I've never felt this before.
I'm going to ask a few quick questions to check your responsiveness. That's standard for patient safety.
What is your full name, and where are you now? Can you tell me the current year and what brought you here?
Your eyes are open and focused, and you're responding clearly. That looks promising.
You answered in full sentences. Let me ask some follow-up about your symptoms.
I'll ask you to squeeze my hand to test for motor response-can you do that for me?
Your airway is patent and clear. I don't see any signs of compromise.
Breathing is normal, no signs of labored breathing. Rate is around 21 respirations per minute.
Your pulse is weak but regular, and skin is pale and cool. Let's take your vital signs.
You mentioned chest pain and radiating to the back. Did you recently vomit?
Have you ever been told you had a heart condition or heart murmur before?
What's the last thing you ate or drank before this started? Did you take any new supplements?
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your chest pain right now?
You appear quite pale and diaphoretic. I don't note any rashes or bruises right now.
I'm going to check your pupils-can you look straight ahead while I shine a light? They appear PERRL.
We didn't detect any unusual chest discomfort when you shifted positions. Let's move to vitals.
Your pulse is 42-slower than normal. Has this ever happened to you before?
Respirations are at 21. Breathing looks steady and unlabored. Keep breathing normally.
Blood pressure is 120 over 66. It's stable for now. We'll check again soon.
Blood sugar is at 115. This is normal. I just repeated the test due to recent symptoms.
Oxygen saturation is 96%. We'll continue to monitor this with our 4-lead EKG soon.
EKG shows sinus bradycardia. Let's do a 12-lead EKG for more detail. Your GCS is 15.
We're applying 4-lead monitoring first. No discomfort reported. This is for EKG accuracy.
We are following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain. Get ready for transport planning.
Your pulse is now 39, respirations have decreased to 14. Blood pressure is 126/67. Glucose is now undetectable.
IV access in the extremity vein was unsuccessful. We'll continue with oxygen and EKG monitoring only for now.
Your chest pain still rates at 7/10? Any change in location or intensity since initial report?
Tell me more about the heart murmur-has it caused issues or needed treatment before?
[COMPANY_NAME] is the ideal facility for this. We'll go there now for further evaluation.
We will now assist you onto the stretcher. Please tell us if you feel any lightheadedness or worsening pain.
Pulse is 41, respiration 16, BP 132/67. EKG still shows bradycardia, oxygen is at 98%.
Vitals are consistent-pulse 41, BP 132/67, O2 at 98%. No new interventions needed at this point.
The supplements you mentioned-did they come from a vitamin, herbal product, or prescription?.
Oxygen saturation increased to 99%. We'll stay on monitoring for any changes during transport.
Secured for transport. I'll make sure all your lines are in place and safe during the drive.
We are on our way to [COMPANY_NAME] trauma bay 8. They'll handle the necessary evaluation upon arrival.
All gear in place and functioning. Please keep us informed if anything changes along the way.
EKG pattern remains stable-no signs of acute cardiac activity. Continue transporting for ER assessment.
Patient is alert, oriented to time, place, and person. EKG is sinus bradycardia with possible left atrial enlargement.
Patient is secured on the [COMPANY_NAME] hot stretcher. Transfer in progress with no incident noted at this time.
BP remains at 132/67, pulse at 41, and O2 98%. You remain comfortable and stable during transport.
We're en route now with vitals monitored. I'll continue to check you periodically during the trip.
EKG pattern is stable-still shows no acute cardiac activity. Transporting for ER assessment at [COMPANY_NAME].
[COMPANY_NAME] trauma bay 8 is our arrival point. They will run the tests needed for final evaluation.
Everything secured. You're all set. Let us know if anything changes before reaching the hospital.
Pulse is 41, BP is 132/67, and spo2 at 98%. No acute cardiac changes noted.
Remaining en route to [COMPANY_NAME] trauma bay for further assessment and treatment.
We're all set for the ride. Please let us know if you feel any lightheadedness or intermittent chest pain.
Vitals stable, no acute requirements. All interventions documented per ePCR.
Patient handed over to trauma nurse at [COMPANY_NAME] Trauma Bay 8. No incident en route. Monitor post-transfer.
We're pulling into the ED now. Trauma nurse is waiting for the final handoff.
